title: Bûche de Noël
subtitle: French Christmas log with chocolate ganache bark
excerpt: A French Christmas log: a thin chocolate sponge rolled around a whipped chocolate ganache, finished with more ganache ridged to look like bark and dusted with icing sugar. Serves eight.
sourceNotes: The bûche de Noël is documented as a French Christmas preparation from the late nineteenth century, deriving from the tradition of the ceremonial Yule log. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: a rolled chocolate sponge filled with ganache is documented in the French Christmas baking chapter. Interwar French domestic cookbooks (pre-1928, public domain) document the bûche de Noël in detail, including the scoring of the exterior ganache to resemble bark and the application of meringue mushrooms as decoration.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Assemble up to 2 days ahead; keep refrigerated. Remove 30 minutes before serving for the ganache to soften.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf; the sponge bakes in 10–12 minutes and should spring back firmly across the whole surface — overbaking makes it crack when rolled

glossaryTerms.definition: The technique for rolling a baked sponge without cracking it. While the sponge is still warm and pliable, it is turned out onto a sheet of baking paper dusted with caster or icing sugar, the paper peeled from the base, and a fresh piece of baking paper placed on top. The sponge is then rolled up with the paper inside, starting from one short edge. This sets the muscle memory of the spiral. Once cool, the paper is unrolled, the filling is spread, and the sponge is rolled again without the paper. Attempting to roll a cold sponge for the first time will crack it.

content.text: The bûche de Noël is a festive assembly project rather than a difficult bake. The sponge is thin and bakes in under 15 minutes; the work is in the rolling and the finishing. Use the
to pre-roll the sponge while warm so it takes the curved shape without resistance.

Bring the {{double-cream}} to a simmer, then pour over the {{dark-chocolate}} in a bowl. Leave for 1 minute, then stir until smooth. Stir in the {{unsalted-butter}}. Cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 1 hour until firm enough to spread and pipe.

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Line a 33 × 23 cm rectangular tin with baking paper. Whisk the {{eggs}} and {{caster-sugar}} in a stand mixer on high speed for 5 minutes until thick, pale, and tripled in volume; the mixture should hold a ribbon for 5 seconds when the beaters are lifted. Sift the {{plain-flour}} and {{cocoa-powder}} together, then sift again over the egg mixture. Fold in with a large metal spoon, cutting through without deflating. Pour into the prepared tin and spread gently to the corners. Bake for 10–12 minutes until the surface springs back firmly when pressed.

Turn the hot sponge onto a sheet of baking paper dusted with icing sugar. Peel off the lining paper. Place a fresh sheet of baking paper on top and roll the sponge up from the short end, paper inside. Leave to cool completely. Remove the ganache from the fridge 10 minutes before using; it should be spreadable but not liquid. Unroll the sponge and spread a generous layer of ganache over the surface, leaving a 2 cm border. Re-roll without the paper. Transfer to a board. Trim the ends on the diagonal to reveal the spiral. Spread the remaining ganache over the exterior and drag a fork along its length to create bark texture. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour, then dust with {{icing-sugar}} before serving.
